target.title: feat: add Skill Workshop proposals and skill research
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds the governed Skill Workshop path for creating and improving OpenClaw skills.

Agents can now use `skill_research` to draft new skills, propose updates to existing skills, revise pending proposals, inspect/list proposals, and move explicit proposals through apply/reject/quarantine actions. The write path is proposal-first: generated skill work is stored as a pending `PROPOSAL.md` draft and only becomes an active `SKILL.md` after the proposal is applied.

## What changed

- Added durable Skill Workshop proposal storage under OpenClaw state, with `proposal.json`, `PROPOSAL.md`, support files, rollback metadata, and a rebuildable `proposals.json` manifest.
- Added the `skill_research` agent tool for create, update, revise, list, inspect, apply, reject, and quarantine actions.
- Added CLI commands under `openclaw skills workshop`.
- Added Gateway proposal methods for list, inspect, create, update, apply, reject, and quarantine flows.
- Added proposal support files under standard skill folders, with text-file validation, scanner checks, hash verification, stale-target detection, and guarded apply writes.
- Added proposal revision support with versioned and dated proposal frontmatter.
- Added system prompt/tool catalog guidance so agents know when to use `skill_research`.
- Added docs and changelog entries for the proposal lifecycle.

## Safety

- Generated skills are pending proposals by default.
- Active skills are not overwritten silently.
- Apply is workspace-scoped and checks stale target state before writing.
- Update proposals preserve target baselines and mark stale when live skill/support files change.
- Support files are validated, scanned, hash-checked, and rejected when unsafe or non-text.
- Apply writes rollback metadata and uses guarded writes to avoid partial or clobbering publishes.
- Reject/quarantine only operate on pending proposals.

## Verification

target.title: fix(agents): recover DeepSeek DSML tool calls
target.body:
Fixes #85918

## Summary

- Recover complete DeepSeek DSML invoke blocks on the OpenAI-compatible completions stream when tools were actually exposed and the invoke name matches a declared tool.
- Preserve the existing DSML visible-text stripping behavior, prefer native `tool_calls` when present, and cap retained DSML bodies.
- Add regression coverage for parameter semantics, split chunks, native-shadow de-dupe, trailing text ordering, disabled/unknown tools, malformed DSML, tool-choice narrowing, structured thinking, and oversized DSML bodies.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: DeepSeek/Foundry completions streams that emit complete DSML tool-call markup as assistant text now become executable tool calls instead of visible/filtered text, while native `tool_calls` remain authoritative.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on this PR branch, rebased onto current `upstream/main`, executed through the OpenAI-compatible completions stream processor with the DeepSeek compatibility path enabled.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with a local OpenClaw stream smoke that feeds a DeepSeek completions chunk containing `<|DSML|tool_calls>` for declared tool `session_status` into `testing.processOpenAICompletionsStream`.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the local OpenClaw stream smoke:

```json
{
  "stopReason": "toolUse",
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "before "
    },
    {
      "type": "toolCall",
      "id": "call_deepseek_dsml_0",
      "name": "session_status",
      "arguments": {
        "sessionKey": "current"
      },
      "partialArgs": "{\"sessionKey\":\"current\"}"
    },
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": " after"
    }
  ],
  "leakedDsml": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The DSML text did not leak into streamed events, the declared `session_status` invocation was recovered as a `toolCall`, surrounding visible text stayed ordered around the recovered call, and the final stop reason changed to `toolUse`.

What was not tested: Live Microsoft Foundry/DeepSeek provider call; no provider credentials used.

target.title: fix(boot): wrap BOOT.md in internal-runtime-context, strip from message-tool args (#53732)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When a primary model fails and a fallback model takes over a boot run, the fallback can echo BOOT.md startup instructions to the user through final replies or through the `message` tool. Reporter @alvaro630 observed this with Gemini-2.5-pro.
- **Why it matters:** This is a prompt-disclosure regression. Fallback attempts preserve the original boot body, and the message-tool path did not previously apply the same internal-runtime-context stripping as final replies.
- **What changed:**
  1. `src/gateway/boot.ts` wraps BOOT.md content in `INTERNAL_RUNTIME_CONTEXT_BEGIN`/`END` with the existing runtime-context notice and escapes user-supplied literal delimiters.
  2. `src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts` strips internal-runtime-context blocks from visible outbound fields before send.
  3. `src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.ts` registers the active boot prompt by session while `runBootOnce` executes, so substantial verbatim BOOT.md echoes that lose the wrapper markers are still suppressed.
  4. Empty sanitized `send` payloads now return a suppressed tool result before `runMessageAction`, avoiding the generic raw-params error log path.
- **What did NOT change:** No config changes, no new permissions, no channel behavior change when no boot/runtime-context echo is present.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #53732
- Pattern precedent: @steipete's `e918e5f75c fix: hide runtime context from submitted prompts` and `6e985a421d fix(webchat): keep runtime context out of visible transcripts`
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- `buildBootPrompt` embedded BOOT.md content as ordinary prompt text.
- `resolveFallbackRetryPrompt` preserves the original body across fallback attempts.
- Final replies already strip internal-runtime-context blocks, but BOOT.md was not wrapped and the message-tool execute path did not strip those blocks before user-visible delivery.
- When sanitization emptied a send, the generic tool failure path could still log original raw params. The latest head suppresses those sends before `runMessageAction`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test files: `src/gateway/boot.test.ts`, `src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.test.ts`, `src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - BOOT.md content is wrapped in internal-runtime-context delimiters and literal user markers are escaped.
  - Active boot prompt context is registered during boot and cleared on success/failure.
  - Message-tool visible fields strip wrapped runtime context and substantial boot echoes.
  - Empty sanitized sends return a suppressed tool result before raw params can be logged.
  - Sends with remaining media/presentation content still proceed after visible text is sanitized.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Fallback-model boot runs no longer leak BOOT.md instructions through tool-mediated channel sends. Normal messages without internal-runtime-context or substantial boot-prompt echoes are unchanged.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Primary model: any model that can fail or overload
- Fallback model: per reporter, Gemini-2.5-pro; deterministic proof uses a loopback OpenAI-compatible provider
- Channel: any channel using the `message` tool send path

### Steps

1. Configure OpenClaw with a primary model plus fallback model.
2. Place a BOOT.md in the workspace with concrete startup instructions.
3. Trigger a boot run with the primary model failing or overloaded.
4. Make the fallback model attempt to send the BOOT.md content through `message(action="send")`.

### Expected

The fallback run may execute BOOT.md intent, but BOOT.md instructions themselves are not delivered to the channel and are not exposed through a raw-params tool error.

### Actual Before This PR

Reporter observed Gemini-2.5-pro copying the BOOT.md prompt section into a message-tool send, leaking startup instructions to the user.

## Evidence

Validation on latest head:

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/gateway/boot.ts src/gateway/boot.test.ts src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.ts src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `env OPENCLAW_TEST_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_HELD=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/gateway/boot.test.ts src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.test.ts`
- `pnpm run tsgo:core`
- `pnpm run tsgo:core:test`

## Human Verification

- Verified the boot wrapper appears with both delimiters and the runtime-context notice.
- Verified user-supplied delimiter strings in BOOT.md are escaped so the wrapper stays unambiguous.
- Verified message-tool sanitization strips reasoning tags, internal-runtime-context blocks, and substantial active boot-prompt echoes.
- Verified empty sanitized sends return `{ status: "suppressed", reason: "internal_runtime_context_echo" }` before `runMessageAction`.
- Verified media/presentation sends still proceed when sanitized text is empty but another send payload remains.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A legitimate message containing OpenClaw internal-runtime-context markers may be stripped. **Mitigation:** This matches the existing final-reply sanitizer tradeoff and uses highly specific marker strings.
- **Risk:** A fallback model paraphrases BOOT.md instead of copying substantial contiguous text. **Mitigation:** The wrapper notice tells models not to repeat internals; the mechanical guard targets the reported verbatim/substantial echo leak class.
- **Risk:** Session-scoped boot context could persist after failed runs. **Mitigation:** `runBootOnce` clears the boot-echo context in `finally`, and tests cover success and failure cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** #53732 fallback-model BOOT.md disclosure through the agent `message` tool. This refreshed proof covers the latest head after the raw-params leak fix.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `dec7b390c43554adf01e7b7e7bfa3ef12f5ef842`, using a real `runBootOnce` invocation, real embedded agent runner, real model-fallback machinery, real `message` tool execution, and real `qa-channel` HTTP bus. Provider was a loopback OpenAI-compatible server so no external model credentials were required.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a temporary `pnpm exec tsx -` harness from the PR worktree. It created isolated state/config/workspace roots, wrote a temp `BOOT.md` containing `BOOT-PROOF-SECRET-75128`, started a loopback OpenAI-compatible provider plus `qa-channel` bus, returned 503 overload for `proof/primary-down`, then returned a fallback `message` tool call from `proof/fallback-echo` that tried to send the BOOT.md secret verbatim.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the latest head:

```text
[agent/embedded] overload profile rotation cap reached for proof/primary-down after 1 rotations; escalating to model fallback
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=proof/primary-down candidate=proof/primary-down reason=overloaded next=proof/fallback-echo detail=503 intentional overloaded primary for fallback proof
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_succeeded requested=proof/primary-down candidate=proof/fallback-echo reason=unknown next=none
Real behavior proof: BOOT.md fallback echo suppression
head: dec7b390c43554adf01e7b7e7bfa3ef12f5ef842
provider calls: primary-down ... -> fallback-echo -> fallback-echo
runBootOnce result: {"status":"ran"}
message tool result count: 1
message tool suppressed result count: 1
message tool raw_params result count: 0
qa-channel outbound messages captured: 0
qa-channel leaked BOOT marker count: 0
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The fallback model reached the real `message` tool and attempted to send the BOOT.md secret. The latest sanitizer returned a suppressed tool result before `runMessageAction`, so no `raw_params` error path logged the original payload, the real `qa-channel` bus captured zero outbound messages, and zero outbound payloads contained `BOOT-PROOF-SECRET-75128`.
- **What was not tested:** No external Gemini-2.5-pro run was performed because this shell has no Gemini/OpenAI credentials. The local proof uses a loopback OpenAI-compatible provider to deterministically force the same primary-failure plus fallback-message-tool leak shape; focused tests cover presentation fields and internal-runtime-context wrapper stripping.

target.title: fix(control-ui): respect chat history max chars config
target.body:
## Summary

This is the intentionally small fix for the Control UI/WebChat truncation bug tracked in #87897.

Control UI was sending every `chat.history` request with an explicit `maxChars: 4000`. The gateway correctly gives an RPC `maxChars` parameter precedence over `gateway.webchat.chatHistoryMaxChars`, so the documented/user-configured setting was ignored whenever history was loaded through Control UI.

This removes the hardcoded Control UI `maxChars` override so `chat.history` can fall back to the gateway's configured/default limit.

Fixes #87897.

## Notes

This is deliberately not the larger full-message-reader work from #84651/#84670. That UI can still be useful later, but it should not block the minimal config-precedence fix: if a user explicitly sets `gateway.webchat.chatHistoryMaxChars`, Control UI should respect it.

## Testing

- `../node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config vitest.config.ts src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` from `ui/`
- `../node_modules/.bin/vite build` from `ui/`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(windows): repair doctor update fallback migration
target.body:
## Summary

- fixes Windows update-mode doctor repair so a stale managed gateway is activated only when the Windows service is already running; stopped services stay staged
- audits stale `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_VERSION` values and repairs version drift through the existing gateway service doctor path
- restarts the installed/running managed gateway after an interactive git update, while respecting externally managed service-repair policy
- removes the legacy Windows Startup-folder fallback only after Task Scheduler reports running evidence, including post-update restart migration

target.title: fix(plugins): scope plugin tool callbacks
target.body:
## Summary

This PR scopes plugin-owned agent tool callbacks to the registering plugin runtime scope.

- Adds `src/plugins/plugin-callback-scope.ts` and wraps `api.registerTool(...)` registrations in `src/plugins/registry.ts`.
- Scopes plugin tool factories, `prepareArguments`, and `execute` callbacks with the registering plugin id/source.
- Covers function factories, static tool objects, array-returned tools, concurrent execution, error restoration, and real bundled plugin paths for `diffs`, `memory-core`, and `llm-task`.
- Deliberately does not add worker/process sandboxing, conversation/session isolation, SDK-global wrapping, or test isolation.

Review focus: `src/plugins/plugin-callback-scope.ts` should keep the ownership boundary tight without adding request-time discovery or changing non-plugin runtime behavior.

## Linked context

Maintainer requested plugin-focused thread isolation only. Things behind the SDK should stay normal.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: plugin-owned tool factories, `prepareArguments`, and `execute` callbacks now run under the owning plugin runtime scope instead of inheriting only the caller's ambient gateway scope.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw PR worktree plus delegated Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts src/plugins/plugin-tool-thread-isolation.e2e.test.ts
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --blacksmith-org openclaw --blacksmith-workflow .github/workflows/ci-check-testbox.yml --blacksmith-job check --blacksmith-ref main --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "corepack pnpm test extensions/diffs/src/tool.test.ts extensions/diffs/src/config.test.ts extensions/diffs/src/render-target.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts extensions/llm-task/src/llm-task-tool.test.ts -- --reporter=dot && corepack pnpm check:changed"
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --blacksmith-org openclaw --blacksmith-workflow .github/workflows/ci-check-testbox.yml --blacksmith-job check --blacksmith-ref main --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "git fetch origin main --deepen=500 && corepack pnpm check:changed"
git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Evidence after fix: focused plugin e2e passed with 6 tests, including bundled `diffs`, `memory-core`, and `llm-task` scope coverage. Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01kswbc3y43gmdjmmw30ffy4z9` / Actions run `26683023773` passed the targeted bundled plugin batch: diffs 59 tests, memory-core 42 tests, llm-task 19 tests. Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01kswbw2t2fsnw4nyy10nm5h24` / Actions run `26683189490` passed `check:changed`. Local autoreview returned clean with no accepted/actionable findings.

Observed result after fix: plugin factories, `prepareArguments`, and `execute` callbacks observed their owning plugin ids/sources; the outer gateway scope was restored after success and after a thrown prepare callback; array factory results were all wrapped. The bundled plugin checks prove `diffs` factory config reads, `memory-core` lazy factory/execute config reads, and `llm-task` execute-time agent runtime calls run under their owning plugin scopes.

What was not tested: live installed third-party plugins, worker/process sandboxing, conversation/session routing isolation, or test-runner isolation. Those are intentionally out of scope.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: the first bundled plugin Testbox command proved the targeted plugin tests, then `check:changed` failed because the delegated checkout lacked an `origin/main...HEAD` merge base. The follow-up Testbox run explicitly fetched `origin/main` with `--deepen=500` and `check:changed` passed.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `No`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`

Highest-risk area: plugin tool callback wrapping.

Risk mitigation: the wrapper only applies to plugin `api.registerTool(...)` callbacks, uses the existing plugin runtime `AsyncLocalStorage` scope helper, preserves the original tool surface, and does not introduce loader workers, conversation/session routing, or new discovery/cache paths.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review.

Still waiting on: PR CI from the latest push.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: local autoreview finding for unscoped `prepareArguments` was fixed and final autoreview is clean.

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene

After investigating the linked issue (#84249), the journal evidence shows the SSH disconnect outage is caused by **SIGTERM** reaching the gateway (via TUI/systemd lifecycle), not SIGHUP. This PR is scoped as **daemon signal hygiene** — ensuring the gateway never crashes on stray SIGHUP (exit 129), which is correct Unix daemon practice regardless of the #84249 outage.

### Changes

- `run-loop.ts`: Gate `process.on('SIGHUP', ...)` behind `detectRespawnSupervisor()`. Only supervised daemons (launchd/systemd/schtasks) ignore SIGHUP; foreground gateways preserve Node.js default behavior (exit 129). Uses the preloaded `eagerLifecycleRuntime` reference for supervisor detection.
- `run-loop.test.ts`: Added mock for `detectRespawnSupervisor`. Two new SIGHUP-specific tests:
  - "ignores SIGHUP when running under a supervisor"
  - "does not install SIGHUP handler in foreground (no supervisor)"
  - Updated existing launchd handoff tests to explicitly mock `detectRespawnSupervisor` return value.
- `docs/cli/gateway.md`: Added **Process Signals** section with behavior table documenting SIGTERM, SIGINT, SIGUSR1, and SIGHUP across foreground and supervised modes.

### Signal behavior table

| Signal | Foreground Gateway | Supervised Gateway (launchd/systemd/schtasks) |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| SIGTERM | Graceful shutdown | Graceful shutdown |
| SIGINT | Graceful shutdown | Graceful shutdown |
| SIGUSR1 | Authorized in-process restart | Authorized in-process restart |
| SIGHUP | No OpenClaw handler; Node.js default (exit 129) | Ignored (daemon survives terminal hangup) |

### HUP compatibility risk: none

Full codebase grep confirms zero `kill -HUP` workflows:
```
$ grep -rn 'kill.*HUP\|kill -1' docs/ scripts/ src/ | grep -v node_modules | grep -v .test.
(zero hits)
```

- Docs define only SIGUSR1=restart, SIGINT/SIGTERM=stop
- `openclaw gateway restart` uses SIGUSR1 internally, not SIGHUP
- Only supervised daemons are affected; foreground behavior is unchanged

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Supervised gateways should ignore SIGHUP (terminal hangup) instead of terminating; foreground gateways should preserve Node.js default SIGHUP termination behavior.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:22-bookworm`, Node.js `v22.22.3`), patched checkout at `bf829f1cd3`, also tested on macOS arm64 host.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Test files authored as part of this PR; SIGHUP handler does not exist on upstream/main. On upstream/main, Node.js terminates on SIGHUP regardless of supervisor context.

### Exact steps and evidence

**Unit tests (37/37 pass)**:
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts

 ✓ cli src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (37 tests) 173ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  37 passed (37)
   Duration  657ms
```

**Live signal delivery — Test A: Supervised mode (SIGHUP ignored)**:
```
[INFO] Supervised gateway simulation started (PID: 205)
[INFO] SIGHUP handler installed (supervisor=launchd)
[INFO] Sending SIGHUP to supervised gateway (PID: 205)...
[PASS] signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (launchd supervised daemon survives terminal hangup)
[PASS] Process survived SIGHUP (expected for supervised mode)
[INFO] Sending second SIGHUP...
[PASS] signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (launchd supervised daemon survives terminal hangup)
[PASS] Process survived second SIGHUP
[INFO] Sending SIGTERM for clean shutdown...
[PASS] SIGTERM received, exiting cleanly
[PASS] Supervised test complete, process exited cleanly on SIGTERM
```

**Live signal delivery — Test B: Foreground mode (Node.js default)**:
```
[INFO] Foreground gateway simulation started (PID: 184)
[INFO] No SIGHUP handler registered
[INFO] Sending SIGHUP to foreground gateway (PID: 184)...
[PASS] Process terminated by SIGHUP (exit code: 129, expected ~129)
```

**Test C: No HUP recycle workflows in codebase**:
```
$ grep -rn 'kill.*HUP\|kill -1' docs/ scripts/ src/ | grep -v node_modules | grep -v .test.
(zero hits)
[PASS] Zero HUP-based recycle workflows found
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Supervised gateways survive multiple SIGHUP signals and exit cleanly only on SIGTERM. Foreground gateways terminate on SIGHUP with exit code 129 (Node.js default behavior preserved).
- **What was not tested**: Live launchd/systemd service-managed gateway was not tested in Docker; the supervisor detection path is exercised via mocked unit tests and the signal handler behavior is verified via live signal delivery.

### Tests

All 37 run-loop tests pass including:
- `ignores SIGHUP when running under a supervisor` (3ms)
- `does not install SIGHUP handler in foreground (no supervisor)` (2ms)

Ref #84249

target.title: fix(skill-workshop): align agent_end hook timeout with max reviewer timeout
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Skill Workshop `agent_end` hook inherits the hook runner's default 30s timeout, but the configured reviewer (`reviewTimeoutMs`) can be up to 180s. When the reviewer takes longer than 30s, the hook times out before completing, blocking auto-capture proposals and logging `timed out after 30000ms` on routine session close.
- Solution: Pass `{ timeoutMs: 210_000 }` (max reviewTimeout + 30s buffer) when registering the `agent_end` hook so the reviewer always has enough budget to finish within the hook contract.
- What changed: `extensions/skill-workshop/index.ts` (1 line — added `{ timeoutMs: 210_000 }` as the third argument to `api.on("agent_end", ...)`), `extensions/skill-workshop/index.test.ts` (+1 regression test).
- What did NOT change: No other components were affected. The hook runner's timeout enforcement, the reviewer timeout config range, and the rest of the Skill Workshop plugin are unchanged.

Fixes #87352

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Skill Workshop `agent_end` hook timed out at 30s while the reviewer timeout can be up to 180s, causing routine session-close timeouts and blocking auto-capture proposals.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.28 from this PR worktree using the repo-backed QA gateway suite on the host runner. The scenario runs against `qa-channel` + `qa-lab` bus + a real gateway child with the `skill-workshop` plugin enabled, `autoCapture: true`, `reviewMode: llm`, `reviewInterval: 1`, and `reviewMinToolCalls: 1`. The provider lane was `mock-openai` so the gateway/session path is real while the model response is deterministic.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  OPENCLAW_QA_ALLOW_LOCALHOST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs qa suite \
    --runner host \
    --provider-mode mock-openai \
    --scenario skill-workshop-reviewer-autonomous \
    --concurrency 1 \
    --output-dir .tmp-qa-skill-workshop

  # Real function-call proof: import plugin, register hooks, assert timeoutMs
  node --import tsx proof_repro.mjs

  # Regression test
  npx vitest run extensions/skill-workshop/index.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
```text
QA suite report: .tmp-qa-skill-workshop/qa-suite-report.md
Started: 2026-05-30T10:35:49.461Z
Finished: 2026-05-30T10:37:31.728Z
Duration ms: 102267
Passed: 1
Failed: 0

Scenario: Skill Workshop reviewer autonomous capture
Status: pass
Step: reviewer creates a reusable skill

---
name: animated-gif-workflow
description: Reusable workflow notes for animated GIF QA tasks.
---

- Confirm the asset has true animation, not a static preview.
- Check dimensions against the target product UI slot.
- Record attribution and license before using the file.
- Keep a local copy under the workspace before integration.
- Re-open the local copy for final verification.

Notes:
- Runs against qa-channel + qa-lab bus + real gateway child + mock-openai provider.
- Scenarios run serially in one gateway worker.

Mechanical log check across suite stdout/report/summary:
- `timed out after 30000ms` was not present.

$ node --import tsx proof_repro.mjs
[HOOK] "before_prompt_build" registered
  handler: function
  opts: undefined
[HOOK] "agent_end" registered
  handler: function
  opts: {"timeoutMs":210000}
  PASS: timeoutMs=210000 >= 180_000

All agent_end hook timeout checks passed.
```
- **Observed result after fix:** The real gateway QA scenario completed the Skill Workshop reviewer auto-capture path after a successful agent session, persisted the `animated-gif-workflow` workspace skill, and did not emit the original `agent_end handler from skill-workshop failed: timed out after 30000ms` timeout. The direct hook-registration proof also shows `agent_end` is registered with `opts.timeoutMs = 210_000`, which exceeds the maximum configurable `reviewTimeoutMs` (180_000). All 23 Skill Workshop vitest tests pass.
- **What was not tested:** The QA run used the deterministic `mock-openai` provider rather than an external live model provider, so no third-party credentials or network model service were required. The original reporter's macOS LaunchAgent setup was not reproduced; this proof exercises the same bundled plugin auto-capture reviewer path through a real OpenClaw gateway child.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test
- Target test file: `extensions/skill-workshop/index.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: The `agent_end` hook registration includes `opts.timeoutMs >= 180_000` (the configured max `reviewTimeoutMs`). This verifies the hook timeout covers the full reviewer time budget.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The test directly asserts the hook registration shape, which would catch any future regression that removes or reduces the timeoutMs option on the agent_end hook.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `api.on("agent_end", handler)` was called without `timeoutMs`, defaulting to the hook runner's 30s (`src/plugins/hooks.ts:194`). The Skill Workshop reviewer (`reviewTimeoutMs`) defaults to 45s and can be configured up to 180s (`extensions/skill-workshop/src/config.ts:42`). When `runEmbeddedAgent` takes longer than 30s, the hook runner times out before the reviewer finishes.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test asserted the `agent_end` hook registration included a timeoutMs value. The new regression test closes this gap.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.

No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

target.title: fix(agents): recover DeepSeek DSML tool markup into synthetic tool calls
target.body:
## Summary
`microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro` can emit DSML tool markup as plain assistant text on the `openai-completions` route instead of native `tool_calls`. This PR adds a conservative recovery path that turns complete, valid DSML `invoke` blocks into synthetic `toolCall` blocks while preserving surrounding visible text and emitting every valid invoke in the response.

This branch now also includes the missing Foundry fallback fix that stops the generic DeepSeek V4 thinking wrapper from injecting unsupported `payload.thinking` on the Foundry-hosted OpenAI-compatible route. Without that, the real CLI proof for the DSML fix was blocked earlier by `400 Unrecognized request argument supplied: thinking` before tool execution could even be exercised.

## What changed
- recover complete DeepSeek DSML `tool_calls` text blocks into synthetic `toolCall` events in `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`
- preserve visible text around recovered DSML markup
- emit every valid recovered `invoke` entry in a DSML block
- keep malformed or incomplete DSML non-executable
- skip the generic DeepSeek V4 thinking wrapper on the `microsoft-foundry` fallback path so the real Foundry route can be exercised without the earlier request-shaping failure
- add focused regression tests for both the DSML recovery behavior and the Foundry fallback-path guard

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: DeepSeek V4 (`microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro`) request-shaping/thinking-wrapper failures on OpenAI-compatible routes, and mixed same-chunk stream processing precedence causing duplicate synthetic + native tool call execution.
- **Real environment tested**: local macOS operator environment, source build of OpenClaw 2026.5.24 (`f52dcddb04`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  Run unit tests verifying same-chunk native tool calls win and suppress synthetic recovery:
  ```bash
  ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t 'skips DSML recovery'
  ```
  Run live one-shot model inference to verify Foundry request execution:
  ```bash
  node openclaw.mjs infer model run --model microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro --prompt "Reply with exactly HELLO_FOUNDRY" --local --json
  ```
- **Evidence**: Copied CLI stdout of successful local test execution and live model run.
- **Observed result**:
  All unit tests for same-chunk precedence passed successfully:
  ```
  ✓ agents-core ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (197 tests | 196 skipped)
  ✓ agents-support ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (197 tests | 196 skipped)
  ```
  Live inference correctly skipped `payload.thinking` and returned `HELLO_FOUNDRY` cleanly:
  ```json
  {
    "ok": true,
    "capability": "model.run",
    "transport": "local",
    "provider": "microsoft-foundry",
    "model": "deepseek-v4-pro",
    "outputs": [
      {
        "text": "HELLO_FOUNDRY",
        "mediaUrl": null
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
- **Not tested**: Direct native `deepseek/...` API endpoints since only the Microsoft Foundry-hosted route is currently configured in this environment.

## Original live evidence for the DSML symptom
Installed runtime checked locally before the patched-build proof work:
- `openclaw --version` -> `OpenClaw 2026.5.20 (e510042)`

Tool-use repro against the real CLI/runtime:
```bash
openclaw agent --local --agent main --model microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro --thinking minimal --message 'Call the session_status tool exactly once with sessionKey current and then stop.' --json
```

Observed assistant payload returned as visible text instead of an executed tool call:
```xml
<｜DSML｜tool_calls>
<｜DSML｜invoke name="session_status">
<｜DSML｜parameter name="sessionKey" string="true">current</｜DSML｜parameter>
</｜DSML｜invoke>
</｜DSML｜tool_calls>
```

A second live variant was malformed/incomplete and omitted the parameter body entirely:
```xml
<｜DSML｜tool_calls>
<｜DSML｜invoke name="session_status">
</｜DSML｜invoke>
</｜DSML｜tool_calls>
```

Subagent/history verification also showed that the tool was not actually executed; the markup was emitted as assistant text.

## Source diagnosis
Relevant repo inspected locally at `/Users/manos/Code/openclaw`.

### What the current code did before this patch
`src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.ts` strips DSML wrapper text from visible output.

`src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` used that filter in `processOpenAICompletionsStream(...)`, but executable tool calls still came only from native `choiceDelta.tool_calls`.

So the prior behavior was:
- if DeepSeek/Foundry emitted native `tool_calls`, OpenClaw could handle them
- if DeepSeek/Foundry emitted DSML tool markup as plain text, OpenClaw filtered/streamed text but did not promote it to `toolCall`

### Why this is likely not just expected model behavior
DeepSeek's own tool-calls docs describe OpenAI-compatible native `message.tool_calls` as the intended path:
- https://api-docs.deepseek.com/guides/tool_calls

There is also upstream evidence that DeepSeek V4 Pro can intermittently emit tool calls as plain text with `tool_calls: null`:
- https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3/issues/1244

So the remaining problem looks like a combination of:
1. upstream/provider instability in DeepSeek tool emission
2. missing defensive recovery in OpenClaw for DSML/textual tool intent on the `openai-completions` stream path

## Validation
Targeted tests run locally:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t 'DeepSeek DSML|tool calls'
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts -t 'DeepSeek V4|MiMo V2.6'
git diff --check
pnpm build
```

Results:
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`: 2 files passed, 18 tests passed, 374 skipped
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts`: 2 files passed, 6 tests passed, 250 skipped
- `git diff --check`: clean
- `pnpm build`: succeeded

## Labels / framing suggestion
- bug
- providers
- microsoft-foundry
- deepseek
- streaming
- tool-calls

target.title: fix(plugins): block untrusted workspace setup-only channel loads
target.body:
## Summary - Problem: latest `main` already keeps execution-facing channel setup discovery on the trusted catalog path, but lower-level setup-only scoped plugin loads could still import and execute disabled workspace channel plugins when callers passed explicit `onlyPluginIds`. - What changed: this PR restores the lower-level security boundary from #64154 by blocking disabled workspace channel plugins before setup-only import, adds a registry backstop that rejects channel registration from disabled workspace plugins, and ports the complementary #85774 guard so a broken trusted setup-entry channel reports a diagnostic instead of aborting sibling plugin loading. - What latest `main` already kept from #64154: `channels add`, onboarding/setup resolution, and setup discovery now use trust-aware catalog helpers; untrusted workspace shadows fall back to bundled/non-workspace entries; workspace-only candidates remain visible only in the setup wizard discovery bucket; the raw catalog listing is kept behind an internal/deprecated helper. - Remaining work before merge: no known #64154 hardening gap remains in code after this PR. The remaining gates are CI plus any maintainer-requested live external-plugin proof. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Replaces/supersedes #64154 - Includes the valuable complementary setup-entry error-isolation fix from #85774 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Current Main vs This PR The #64154 design had two layers: 1. Catalog callers should resolve channels through trusted helpers so setup/add flows do not select untrusted workspace shadows. 2. The loader/registry should enforce the same trust boundary before any plugin module can execute, so lower-level callers cannot bypass the catalog layer. Latest `main` still preserves the first layer. `channels add`, setup/onboarding, and channel setup plugin resolution use `listTrustedChannelPluginCatalogEntries` / `getTrustedChannelPluginCatalogEntry`; the setup wizard's installable display can still show workspace-only candidates through the lenient discovery helper without treating them as executable/installed. This PR fills the second layer. It prevents explicit setup-only scoped loads from importing disabled workspace channel plugins, rejects disabled workspace channel registrations at the registry boundary, and keeps trusted workspace overrides working. It also ports #85774's try/catch around `api.registerChannel(mergedSetupPlugin)`, which addresses a related but distinct resilience gap: trusted setup-entry plugins are allowed to load, but one malformed registration should not stop healthy sibling plugins from loading. ## Diagram ```mermaid graph TD SOURCES["Plugin source directories"] --> MANIFEST["Manifest registry
(pluginId + origin + channels)"] MANIFEST -->|"setup / onboard / channels add"| TRUSTED["Trusted catalog helpers
strict execution list
untrusted workspace shadows fall back"] MANIFEST -->|"setup wizard display"| DISCOVERY["Setup discovery helper
lenient display list
workspace-only candidates visible"] MANIFEST -->|"gateway or explicit scoped setup load"| LOADER["loadOpenClawPlugins()"] TRUSTED --> SETUP["setup/add snapshot request"] DISCOVERY --> UI["installable UI bucket only
not an execution decision"] SETUP --> LOADER LOADER --> GATE{"This PR:
workspace channel plugin enabled?"} GATE -->|"disabled workspace"| BLOCK["reject before setup-only import
no plugin code executes"] GATE -->|"trusted workspace or non-workspace"| IMPORT["import setup/runtime entry"] IMPORT --> REGISTER{"This PR + #85774:
registerChannel succeeds?"} REGISTER -->|"yes"| REGISTRY["snapshot/runtime registry"] REGISTER -->|"throws"| DIAG["record plugin diagnostic
continue loading siblings"] REGISTRY --> SNAPUSE["validateInput / applyAccountConfig
write config / lifecycle callbacks"] REGISTRY --> RUNTIME["gateway/runtime channel lookup"] BLOCK:::fixed GATE:::fixed DIAG:::fixed TRUSTED:::kept DISCOVERY:::display UI:::display classDef fixed fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px classDef kept fill:#d9edf7,stroke:#31708f,stroke-width:2px classDef display fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px ``` The important boundary is now shared: catalog helpers decide which channel catalog entries are trusted for setup/add flows, and the loader/registry enforce the same rule before runtime code can execute. If a trusted setup-entry channel still fails during registration, the error is isolated to that plugin and sibling plugins continue loading. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: untrusted workspace channel plugins should not execute during setup-only scoped loads; disabled workspace channel registrations should be rejected at registry assembly; trusted setup-entry channel registration failures should not abort sibling plugin loading. - Real environment tested: local Linux repo checkout rebased on `upstream/main` `717003aaffec3d41792424079a52beaf2bd92a5c`; PR head `55811ad9edce27d49decb99c7564fba5c4138fb3`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: - `pnpm docs:list` - `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-channels.e2e.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` - `pnpm test src/plugins/loader.test.ts src/plugins/registry.channel-guard.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/channel-plugin-resolution.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/discovery.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/workspace-shadow-bypass.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/plugin-install.test.ts src/commands/channels.add.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` - `pnpm build` - `pnpm check:changed` - Evidence after fix: `src/commands/onboard-channels.e2e.test.ts` passes 16/16 after exposing `listRawChannelPluginCatalogEntries` through the same mock/actual fallback as the old catalog helper; the focused loader/registry/catalog/channel setup tests pass after the rebase; `pnpm build` and the final `pnpm check:changed` pass; `CHANGELOG.md` and stale transcripts tool-display metadata are no longer in the final branch diff. - Observed result after fix: disabled workspace shadows no longer execute through scoped setup-only loads; trusted workspace overrides remain available; disabled workspace channel registrations are rejected as a registry backstop; malformed trusted setup-entry channel registration records a diagnostic and sibling plugins keep loading. - What was not tested: live channel setup against a real external workspace plugin outside the loader test harness. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the same minimal setup-only repro failed on latest `origin/main` before this branch because the marker file was created during scoped setup-only load. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: the scoped setup-only loader path treated explicit `onlyPluginIds` as sufficient to load a disabled workspace channel plugin, so workspace trust was bypassed below the trusted-catalog call sites. - Missing detection / guardrail: registry assembly still accepted channel registration from disabled workspace plugins if a lower-level caller imported one. - Complementary #85774 root cause: the setup-entry loader loop caught surrounding registration failures, but `api.registerChannel(mergedSetupPlugin)` itself was unguarded, so one throwing trusted channel registration could abort sibling plugin loading. - Contributing context: latest `main` already absorbed the higher-level `channels add` / trusted-catalog call-site fixes from #64154, but not this lower-level loader/registry backstop or the setup-entry error-isolation guard. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `src/plugins/loader.test.ts`, `src/plugins/registry.channel-guard.test.ts`, `src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts`, and existing channel setup/add tests. - Scenario the test should lock in: explicit scoped setup-only loads must reject disabled workspace channel plugins but keep trusted workspace channel plugins available; registry assembly must reject disabled workspace channel registrations; trusted setup-entry registration errors must be recorded without aborting healthy sibling plugins. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bugs live at loader/registry/catalog boundaries and require real plugin import behavior plus registration-mode assertions. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): `only setup-loads a disabled channel plugin when the caller scopes to the selected plugin` still covers the non-workspace path. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Untrusted workspace channel shadows no longer execute during setup-only scoped loads. - Trusted workspace channel plugins remain available to setup-time scoped loading. - A broken trusted setup-entry channel registration now surfaces as a plugin diagnostic instead of stopping sibling plugin loading. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Linux - Runtime/container: Node 22 local repo checkout - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): plugin loader / channel plugin registry / channel setup catalog - Relevant config (redacted): workspace plugins enabled; untrusted plugin omitted from allowlist ### Steps 1. Create a workspace channel plugin fixture with `channels` metadata and a side-effect marker write in `register()`. 2. Load it through `loadOpenClawPlugins({ includeSetupOnlyChannelPlugins: true, forceSetupOnlyChannelPlugins: true, onlyPluginIds: [...] })`. 3. Compare disabled workspace behavior against trusted workspace behavior. 4. Load a setup-entry channel whose channel config getter throws during `registerChannel` alongside a healthy sibling plugin. ### Expected - Disabled workspace channel plugin stays disabled and does not execute. - Trusted workspace channel plugin remains available to setup-only loading. - A throwing trusted setup-entry channel records a diagnostic and does not prevent sibling plugins from loading. ### Actual - This branch matches the expected behavior. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets in test output - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: untrusted/trusted workspace scoped setup-only regressions, disabled workspace registry guard, trusted-catalog filtering semantics, onboarding channel setup e2e catalog mock compatibility, `channels add` workspace shadow fallback, #85774 setup-entry `registerChannel` error isolation, `pnpm build`, and final `pnpm check:changed`. - Edge cases checked: trusted workspace override still loads in setup-only mode; non-workspace disabled plugin behavior stays unchanged; setup wizard discovery still keeps workspace-only install candidates visible without treating them as installed/executable. - What you did **not** verify: a live end-to-end channel setup outside the loader test harness. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) No. This is an intentional security hardening break for disabled workspace channel plugins that were previously reachable through setup-only scoped loads. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No automatic migration. Operators who intentionally develop or run a workspace/linked channel plugin must explicitly enable/trust that plugin before setup-time code can run. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: use the normal plugin enable/trust flow for the workspace plugin before running channel setup. The behavior is documented in `docs/cli/plugins.md`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: a trusted workspace plugin could accidentally get blocked if the trust check were too broad. - Mitigation: regression coverage explicitly keeps the trusted workspace scoped-load path green. - Risk: setup discovery could accidentally hide workspace-only install candidates. - Mitigation: trusted-catalog and discovery tests keep the strict execution list separate from the lenient setup wizard display list. - Risk: an existing disabled workspace channel plugin that relied on setup-only execution now fails closed. - Mitigation: this is the intended security behavior; the docs now state that workspace-origin plugins are not imported or executed until explicitly enabled/trusted. ### Proof 1: Pre-Fix Reproduction on origin/main - Behavior addressed: Untrusted workspace channel plugin (evi1-channel-probe) executes its register() function via the setup-only scoped load path when includeSetupOnlyChannelPlugins: true is passed to loadOpenClawPlugins, bypassing the trust boundary. - Real environment tested: Crabbox docker, provider=docker, container=391883b7652bcb464c523f64ddcabb9bcad47f92e434737b7cc5391f7bc0922f, run=cbx_6dadb5d505eb, checkout SHA=ead847f6065ac36031f9e7a95dd1ee90f171f79c (origin/main) - Exact steps or command run after this patch: (1) `git fetch --depth=1 origin main && git checkout FETCH_HEAD` inside Crabbox container; (2) `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`; (3) Deploy evi1-channel-probe plugin to /tmp/proof1-workspace/.openclaw/extensions/evi1-channel-probe/; (4) Confirm /tmp/openclaw-marker-evidence absent; (5) Run `node probe-untrusted.mjs` with onlyPluginIds=["evi1-channel-probe"], includeSetupOnlyChannelPlugins=true, activate=false, cache=false; (6) Check /tmp/openclaw-marker-evidence existence. - Evidence after fix: Marker file /tmp/openclaw-marker-evidence CREATED with content "evi1-probe-executed\n". Plugin status="disabled" with channelSetups=[{id:"evi1-channel-probe"}] — the setup-only path imported and executed the untrusted workspace plugin code. - Observed result after fix: On origin/main (pre-fix), the untrusted workspace channel plugin code ran despite being disabled. The plugin appeared in channelSetups (not channels), status was "disabled", yet register() executed and created the marker file. This confirms the bypass vulnerability. - What was not tested: CLI blackbox path, real channels add flow ### Proof 2: Post-Fix Verification on PR Head - Behavior addressed: The PR fix blocks untrusted workspace channel plugins from executing during setup-only scoped loads; disabled workspace channel registrations are rejected at registry assembly; trusted workspace plugins with plugins.allow still work; broken trusted setup-entry registerChannel records a diagnostic without aborting sibling plugins. - Real environment tested: Crabbox docker, provider=docker, container=df29879e2faef7ac8794cba519c1988aff410316a3718d90c6f638cd9977ce15, lease=cbx_0f687f5da081, checkout SHA=b05a7b14fe9bd6c119b3b7fe60a1967d0987804c (PR head at time of test; refreshed #86953 head is `2a9a2d79fd498be5fc62e83634a544f873b67b4c`) - Exact steps or command run after this patch: (1) `git fetch origin pull/82579/head:pr-head && git checkout pr-head` inside Crabbox container; (2) `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`; (3) `grep -r "channel registration rejected for disabled workspace plugin" dist/` — fix code found in dist/loader-DM-dphfh.js; (4) Deploy evi1-channel-probe (untrusted), confirm marker ABSENT after probe; (5) Deploy evi1-trusted-probe (with plugins.allow), confirm marker EXISTS; (6) Deploy evi1-broken-register + evi1-sibling-probe (with plugins.allow), confirm sibling marker EXISTS and diagnostic recorded; (7) Run focused tests: `pnpm test src/plugins/loader.test.ts` (142 pass), `pnpm test src/plugins/registry.channel-guard.test.ts` (2 pass), `pnpm test src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts` (5 pass), `pnpm test src/commands/channel-setup/workspace-shadow-bypass.test.ts` (6 pass); (8) `pnpm check:changed` — 0 errors. - Evidence after fix: Untrusted probe: MARKER_ABSENT, exit code 0 (clean block, not crash). Plugin status="disabled", channelSetups=[]. Trusted probe: MARKER_EXISTS, content "evi1-probe-executed\n". Plugin status="loaded", channels and channelSetups populated. Error isolation: broken-register records diagnostic (level=error, pluginId=evi1-broken-register), process exit code 0. Sibling marker EXISTS, content "evi1-sibling-probe-executed\n". - Observed result after fix: The fix blocks untrusted workspace channel plugin execution. Untrusted plugin stays disabled with empty channelSetups. Trusted workspace plugin with plugins.allow still loads and registers channels. Broken trusted registerChannel records a diagnostic and sibling plugins continue loading. All focused tests pass. - What was not tested: CLI blackbox path, real channels add flow, 3 pre-existing test failures in channel-auth.test.ts (2) and channels.remove.test.ts (1) unrelated to this PR Crabbox cross-reference: Proof 1 provider=docker, container=391883b7652bcb464c523f64ddcabb9bcad47f92e434737b7cc5391f7bc0922f, run=cbx_6dadb5d505eb, SHA=ead847f6065ac36031f9e7a95dd1ee90f171f79c (origin/main). Proof 2 provider=docker, container=df29879e2faef7ac8794cba519c1988aff410316a3718d90c6f638cd9977ce15, lease=cbx_0f687f5da081, SHA=b05a7b14fe9bd6c119b3b7fe60a1967d0987804c (#82579 head; superseded by #86953, refreshed head `2a9a2d79fd498be5fc62e83634a544f873b67b4c`). Proof 1 SHA (ead847f6) is an ancestor of Proof 2 SHA (b05a7b14fe) via the main branch lineage. Probe infrastructure file SHA-256 hashes are identical between both proofs (VAL-CROSS-01 confirmed).

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`
